summary: French intuitionist philosopher known for the concept of duration (qualitative, non-spatialized experience of time), the elan vital (creative impulse driving evolution), and his works Time and Free Will, Creative Evolution, and Matter and Memory. Won the Nobel Prize in Literature. Debated Albert Einstein on the nature of time in 1922.

works.name: Duration and Time
works.description: Bergson's central philosophical concept: duration (duree) is the subjective, qualitative experience of time, distinct from the mechanistic 'clock time' of science. Duration is a qualitative rather than quantitative multiplicity, understood as non-linear continuum that cannot be broken into instances. He illustrated it with several images: two spools with a tape running between them, a color spectrum, and the stretching of an infinitely small elastic band. Duration was introduced in his doctoral thesis Time and Free Will, subtitled 'An Essay on the Immediate Data of Consciousness.' He famously debated Albert Einstein in 1922, arguing against removing the human experience aspect from time and publishing Duration and Simultaneity in response to Einstein's claim that 'the time of the philosophers does not exist.' Bergson described relativity as 'a metaphysics grafted upon science.'

works.name: Creative Evolution and Elan Vital
works.description: Bergson's 1907 work arguing that a force called elan vital (vital impulse) fuels the development of organisms. This creative impulse drives life forward as a hypothetical reasoning for organism development. Leopold Senghor likened the Bantu concept of 'Vital Force' (from Placide Tempels) to Bergson's elan vital. Gilles Deleuze wrote Bergsonism to explain the relationship between duration, memory, and elan vital in Bergson's work.

works.name: Matter and Memory
works.description: Bergson's work on the relationship between mind and body, which uses the image of an inverted cone inserted into a plane (with the summit representing present perception) to describe how the past affects perception of the present to varying degrees. He distinguished between sensori-motor 'habit-memory' and unconscious 'pure-memory.' He argued that memory is not localized in a specific part of the brain, written as a response to Theodule Ribot.

works.name: Laughter
works.description: Bergson's 1900 book (or essay collection), the first notable philosophical work on humor. He argues that the comic is a strictly human phenomenon, that when humans make fun of animals they recognize human behavior in them, and that the methods of comedy are repetition, inversion, and reciprocal interference.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: French intuitionist philosopher who won the Nobel Prize in Literature. His doctoral thesis is Time and Free Will, subtitled 'An Essay on the Immediate Data of Consciousness.' He also wrote The Two Sources of Morality and Religion (dividing morality into 'open' and 'closed' forms corresponding to 'dynamic' and 'static' religion). Broadway's first traffic jam was reportedly caused by one of his lectures. Keiji Nishitani wrote a dissertation on 'the Real and the Ideal' in the thought of Schelling and Bergson. Criticized Plato's theory of changeless 'Ideas' in a discussion of the dialogue Parmenides. A chapter about Bergson and Einstein was omitted from the American edition of Sokal and Bricmont's Fashionable Nonsense. Deleuze discussed Riemann's theory of discrete and continuous multiplicities in relation to both Bergson and Einstein.

comprehensive_summary:
Henri Bergson is a French intuitionist philosopher best known for his concept of duration (duree), the elan vital, and his works Time and Free Will, Creative Evolution, and Matter and Memory. He won the Nobel Prize in Literature.

Bergson's central concept is duration — the subjective, qualitative experience of time, distinct from the mechanistic clock time of science. Duration is understood as a qualitative rather than quantitative multiplicity, a non-linear continuum that cannot be broken into discrete instances. He illustrated it with several vivid images: two spools with a tape running between them, a color spectrum, and the stretching of an infinitely small elastic band. Duration was introduced in his doctoral thesis Time and Free Will, subtitled 'An Essay on the Immediate Data of Consciousness.' In 1922, Bergson famously debated Albert Einstein, publishing Duration and Simultaneity to rebut Einstein's claim that 'the time of the philosophers does not exist.' Bergson described Einstein's theory of relativity as 'a metaphysics grafted upon science.' A 2015 book by Jimena Canales explores this debate, and a chapter about Bergson and Einstein was omitted from the American edition of Sokal and Bricmont's Fashionable Nonsense.

Creative Evolution (1907) argues that a force called elan vital (vital impulse) fuels the development of organisms, proposed as a creative impulse that drives life forward. Leopold Senghor likened the Bantu concept of 'Vital Force' to Bergson's elan vital. Gilles Deleuze wrote Bergsonism to explain the relationships between duration, memory, and elan vital, and also discussed Riemann's theory of discrete and continuous multiplicities in relation to Bergson and Einstein.

Matter and Memory examines the relationship between mind and body, using the image of an inverted cone inserted into a plane (with the summit representing present perception) to describe how the past affects perception of the present to varying degrees. Bergson distinguished between sensori-motor 'habit-memory' and unconscious 'pure-memory,' and argued that memory is not localized in a specific part of the brain, written in response to Theodule Ribot.

Laughter (1900) is the first notable philosophical work on humor, arguing that the comic is a strictly human phenomenon and that when we make fun of animals, we recognize human behavior in them. The methods of comedy are identified as repetition, inversion, and reciprocal interference.

Bergson's final book, The Two Sources of Morality and Religion, divides morality into 'open' and 'closed' forms corresponding to 'dynamic' and 'static' religion. Broadway's first traffic jam was reportedly caused by one of his lectures. Keiji Nishitani wrote a dissertation on 'the Real and the Ideal' in the thought of Schelling and Bergson. Bergson criticized Plato's theory of changeless 'Ideas' in a discussion of the dialogue Parmenides.

cards.front: Concept: this philosopher's central idea of subjective time experience is a qualitative rather than quantitative multiplicity, not spatialized or broken into instances
Philosopher: illustrated duration with the image of two spools with a tape running between them, a color spectrum, and an infinitely small elastic band
Philosopher: debated Einstein in 1922; published Duration and Simultaneity; called relativity 'a metaphysics grafted upon science, though not science'
Concept: a creative impulse that drives the development of organisms forward, proposed in the 1907 book Creative Evolution
Philosopher: Leopold Senghor likened the Bantu concept of 'Vital Force' to this thinker's concept of élan vital
Work: uses the image of an inverted cone inserted into a plane, with the summit representing present perception, to describe how the past affects perception to varying degrees
Work: distinguishes sensori-motor 'habit-memory' from unconscious 'pure-memory'; argues memory is not localized in a specific brain region
Work: the first notable philosophical work on humor (1900); argues the comic is strictly human and identifies the methods of comedy as repetition, inversion, and reciprocal interference
Work: divides morality into 'open' and 'closed' forms corresponding to 'dynamic' and 'static' religion
cards.back: The Two Sources of Morality and Religion (Bergson)

cards.front: Philosopher: Broadway's first traffic jam was reportedly caused by a lecture given by this thinker
Philosopher: Gilles Deleuze wrote a book about this thinker exploring the relationship between duration, memory, and élan vital
